bar.name: Seven Grand
bar.address: 515 W 7th St, 2nd Floor, Los Angeles, CA 90014
bar.neighborhood: DTLA (Downtown Los Angeles) — Financial District / Jewelry District
bar.vibe: Dimly lit whiskey lodge with taxidermy, dark wood paneling, vintage jewelry display cases repurposed as whiskey showcases, hunting-lodge-meets-speakeasy atmosphere, pool table, live DJs on weekends, hidden bar (The Bar at Seven Grand / Jackalope) behind a bookcase
barFacts: Opened April 6, 2007 by Cedd Moses (213 Nightlife / Pouring with Heart) — signaled the rebirth of craft cocktails in DTLA
Named for the intersection of 7th Street and Grand Avenue
Housed in the 1922 Brock & Company Jewelry Emporium building (HCM #358) — vintage jewelry cases now display 900+ whiskeys
After Brock's closed, the space became Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria in 1974 — the taxidermy aesthetic comes from that lineage
Moses' original rent for the entire 25,000 sq ft space: $1,600/month
Inspired by The Stags' Head pub in Dublin — founder Cedd Moses wanted to replicate its warmth in LA
Hidden bar Jackalope accessible through a bookcase door — speakeasy within a whiskey bar
Second location opened in San Diego's North Park in 2012

route.direction: West along 7th Street — Broadway to Grand

pois.name: Los Angeles Theatre
facts: Last and most extravagant of the 12 ornate movie palaces built on Broadway between 1911 and 1931
Opened January 30, 1931 with the premiere of Charlie Chaplin's 'City Lights' — Chaplin's guests that night: Albert Einstein and his wife Elsa
Chaplin helped finance completion of the theatre so it could open in time for his premiere
Designed by S. Charles Lee and Samuel Tilden Norton in French Baroque style — interior modeled after the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles
Built in less than 6 months — hole in the ground in August 1930, grand opening January 1931
2,000-seat auditorium with working fountain in the lobby, children's playroom, full restaurant and ballroom, crying rooms for mothers, radio broadcast studio
Lee installed a periscope and prism system to relay the film image into the basement lounges
Electric seat indicators for ushers — cutting-edge tech in 1931
Part of the Broadway Theater and Commercial District — first and largest theater district listed on the National Register (1979)

features.detail: French Baroque facade with ornate terra cotta detailing on Broadway frontage
Grand marquee projecting over sidewalk
Elaborate lobby with working fountain visible through doors

features.element: Movie palaces on Broadway between 3rd and 9th streets

connections.link: Financed completion and premiered 'City Lights' here with Einstein in attendance
Both built during the peak of DTLA's pre-war golden era
Sits at the exact intersection (7th & Broadway) that was the western terminus of Route 66 from 1926-1936
Two parallel streets — entertainment on Broadway, money on Spring — that powered DTLA's golden age

pois.name: Original Western Terminus of Route 66
facts: From 1926 to 1936, this intersection was the official western terminus of US Route 66 — where the 2,448-mile road from Chicago ended
In 1924, this intersection saw 504,000 pedestrians per day — one of the busiest in the world
The route inherited the path of the National Old Trails Road (NOTR), established 1912
In 1936, the terminus was extended to Santa Monica — the Santa Monica pier 'End of the Trail' sign came later
Historical marker erected 2003 by Hampton Hotels in the sidewalk
Thousands of Dust Bowl migrants and dreamers ended their westward journey at this exact corner

connections.link: Chaplin's premiere happened steps from where Route 66 ended — every traveler's first view of LA glamour
The road that brought America to LA ended in the middle of its theater district

pois.name: James Oviatt Building
facts: Built 1928 for haberdasher James Oviatt — LA's most fashionable men's clothier

Oviatt visited the 1925 Exposition Internationale des Arts Decoratifs in Paris and was so stunned by Rene Lalique's glass pavilion that he commissioned the artisan's first-ever commercial installation
30 tons of Lalique glass shipped from France — the largest commercial Lalique installation ever undertaken
Front doors: two angels holding a California mission bell, each door 7.5 feet tall — believed to be the largest panels of lead crystal Lalique ever produced
Elevator doors feature Lalique glass carved with foliage and fruit
Lalique created a glass ceiling and massive chandelier with arms resembling palm fronds for the main shopping space
Penthouse featured: Turkish bath, gym, tennis court, practice golf links, rooftop garden, pool with sand imported from France for a private beach
Penthouse decorated by Parisian firm Saddler et fils — burled mahogany, parquet floors in geometric patterns
Designed by Walker & Eisen — excavation August 1927, completed May 1928
Oviatt was later accused of Nazi sympathies — reportedly attended German-American Bund meetings in the 1930s

features.detail: Art Deco facade with geometric ornamentation
Lalique glass entrance doors — two angels holding a mission bell
Ornate lobby with Lalique glass ceiling and chandelier

features.element: Tons of Lalique glass in the building

features.detail: 12-story building, height approximately 58.5 meters (Overpass data)

connections.link: One visit to the Paris expo that invented Art Deco changed the LA skyline — Oviatt brought Lalique to America
Both luxury retail empires on 7th Street — Oviatt sold suits, Brock sold jewels
Two buildings within 2 blocks where a single artisan's work defines the entire structure
Oviatt's building sits at the edge of the 'Wall Street of the West' — the clothier dressed the bankers

pois.name: Clifton's Cafeteria (Clifton's Republic)
facts: Founded 1931 by Clifford Clinton during the Great Depression as 'a poor man's nightclub'
Business policy printed on every check: 'Regardless of the amount of this check, our cashier will cheerfully accept whatever you wish to pay — or you may dine free'
During a 90-day period, 10,000 people ate for free
Interior transformed by rock sculptor Francois Scotti: 20-foot waterfall, faux redwood trees concealing steel columns, a sylvan wonderland inspired by Santa Cruz Mountains
Current 40-foot-tall fake redwood tree installed during 2015 renovation — branches reinforced to hold aerialists
Clinton became a civic crusader — helped bust the corrupt Shaw-Parrot administration (LAPD corruption ring) in the late 1930s. A bomb was planted in his basement in retaliation
Building originally opened 1913 as Boos Brothers Cafeteria
Clinton family: five generations as California restaurateurs, starting 1888 when David Harrison Clinton bought the Southern Hotel dining room
Clifton's Silver Spoon — a second cafeteria — later occupied the Brock & Co building at 515 W 7th (now Seven Grand)

features.detail: Multi-story facade with forest/nature theming visible from street
40-foot fake redwood tree visible through windows

connections.link: Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria occupied the Brock building from 1974 until Seven Grand opened — the taxidermy in Seven Grand descends from Clifton's aesthetic
Fed 10,000 people free during the Depression, then fought city hall corruption — a bomb in his basement was the price
Both Depression-era openings on Broadway — entertainment and food for a city in crisis

pois.name: Brock & Company Building (Seven Grand)
facts: Built 1922 for George A. Brock's jewelry emporium — LA's most important jewelry and giftware store
Brock & Co. founded 1903 (as Brock & Feagans), officially 1911
Designed by Dodd and Richards — four-story Spanish Colonial Revival with engaged columns, stacked floral motifs, and a swan's neck broken pediment at the parapet
Brock signed a 99-year lease on the property
In 1923, Brock merged with Simon Nordlinger & Co., which had operated since 1870 on Commercial Street
Ben Weingart bought Brock & Co. in 1964
Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria moved into the space in 1974 after Brock's lost its lease
Cedd Moses opened Seven Grand in the building April 6, 2007 — original rent: $1,600/month for 25,000 sq ft
Vintage Brock & Co. jewelry display cases repurposed to showcase 900+ whiskeys

features.detail: Spanish Colonial Revival facade — thin engaged columns, stacked floral motifs
Swan's neck broken pediment at the parapet line
Vintage jewelry display cases visible inside, now holding whiskey bottles

connections.link: Clifton's Silver Spoon occupied this space 1974-2007 — the taxidermy connection
Both anchored the westward expansion of DTLA's commercial district along 7th Street
Same display cases, different treasure — jewels became whiskey

pois.name: Spring Street Financial District ('Wall Street of the West')
pois.address: S Spring St between 4th and 7th Streets
designations: National Register of Historic Places — Spring Street Financial District
facts: Nicknamed 'Wall Street of the West' — financial center of Los Angeles through the first half of the 20th century
23 financial structures plus 3 hotels along S Spring St from just north of 4th to just south of 7th
Named 'Spring Street' by city surveyor E.O.C. Ord in 1849 — he was wooing a woman named Trinidad de la Guerra and called her 'mi primavera, my springtime'
Headquarters for Bank of America, Farmers and Merchants Bank, Crocker National Bank, California Bank & Trust, International Savings & Exchange Bank
At least 10 buildings designed by John Parkinson, who later designed City Hall and Union Station
Hellman and Continental Buildings (1902) were the first major structures to anchor the district
Beaux Arts facades remain virtually intact — the district is a popular film shooting location for period productions
Now known as 'Gallery Row' as art galleries moved into the old bank buildings, many converted to lofts

features.detail: Continuous Beaux Arts facades along both sides of Spring Street

features.element: Historic financial structures in the district

features.detail: Ornate terra cotta detailing, classical columns, and elaborate cornices on multiple buildings

features.text: Various bank names carved into building facades

connections.link: Two parallel streets — the money and the show — that powered DTLA's golden age
Designed at least 10 buildings on this one street, plus City Hall and Union Station
Oviatt dressed the bankers who worked on Spring Street — his building sits one block west

pois.name: St. Vincent Court
pois.address: Alley between Broadway and Hill, 6th and 7th Streets
facts: Hidden alley named for St. Vincent's College (now Loyola University) — first institution of higher learning in Southern California, founded 1865 by Vincentian Fathers
This was the site of Saint Vincent's College from 1868 to 1887
Became Bullock's department store delivery alley when the massive store opened next door
In 1957, the plain alleyway was decorated like an ancient European lane — plaster facades, brick paving, Italian restaurants and espresso bars
When Bullock's closed in 1983, the complex became St. Vincent Jewelry Center — 'the largest jewelry collective in the world'
Now home to mostly Middle Eastern restaurants — feels like Rome or Florence, not downtown LA
The journey: Jesuit college (1868) → delivery alley → Italian restaurants (1957) → jewelry collective (1983) → kebab corridor (today)

features.detail: Narrow brick-paved alley with European-style plaster facades
Feels like a European courtyard hidden behind modern storefronts

connections.link: The delivery alley for the biggest department store on 7th Street
Both are concealed spaces in plain sight — one behind modern storefronts, one behind a bookcase
Both established within 2 years — among the oldest civic institutions in LA

pois.name: Pershing Square
facts: Original pueblo lands — ownership traceable to 1781, when Spain granted them to the City of Los Angeles
Dedicated 1866 by Mayor Cristobal Aguilar as 'La Plaza Abaja' — LA's oldest public park
Renamed for General John J. Pershing in 1918
Contains LA's oldest public art sculpture: the Spanish American War Memorial (1900) by Samuel M. Goddard — first piece of public art in the city

Beethoven statue (1932) by Arnold Foerster — commissioned by William Andrews Clark Jr., founder of the LA Philharmonic, to honor his favorite composer. A man named T.P. Hull protested: no statues of foreigners in an American park
WWI 'Doughboy' statue (1924) by Humberto Pedretti
1910 redesign by John Parkinson (who also designed City Hall and Union Station) — three-tier fountain with four life-size concrete cherubs
$14.5M 1994 redesign by Mexican architect Ricardo Legorreta: 10-story purple bell tower (campanile), earthquake fault line walkway by artist Barbara McCarren
Currently under three-phase renovation for the 2028 Summer Olympics

features.detail: 10-story purple bell tower (campanile)
Beethoven statue on marble base
Spanish American War Memorial (1900) — LA's oldest public art
WWI Doughboy statue (1924)
Walkway representing an earthquake fault line by Barbara McCarren

connections.link: Parkinson redesigned the park in 1910, designed Spring Street banks, plus City Hall and Union Station
Two of LA's oldest civic institutions, 2 blocks apart
LA's oldest public sculpture garden vs. the world's largest private Lalique installation — 2 blocks apart

pois.name: Bank of Italy Building (Hotel Per La)
facts: 12-story Neoclassical building completed 1922 — LA headquarters of the Bank of Italy, forerunner to Bank of America
Founded by Amadeo Giannini, who built Bank of America into the world's largest bank by lending to immigrants and small businesses that other banks wouldn't touch
Designed by Morgan, Walls & Clements in Neoclassical style — Doric columns, ornate golden ceiling, marble floors
Bronze front doors surrounded by terra cotta sculptures of American coins
Colonnade at the top for extra visibility of classical imagery — the building was designed to communicate power and continuity
Converted to Hotel Per La (now Hotel Per La, formerly NoMad LA) in 2018
Directly adjacent to Seven Grand's building — the banker and the jeweler shared a property line

features.detail: 12-story Neoclassical facade with Doric columns at top
Bronze front doors with terra cotta coin sculptures
Colonnade at top stories

connections.link: Directly adjacent — the banker and the jeweler shared a property line on 7th Street
Giannini built Bank of America by lending to immigrants other banks rejected — the building is a monument to that bet
Built the same year, on the same block — finance and luxury retail growing in lockstep

pois.name: Old Warner Bros. Theatre (Downtown Jewelry Exchange)
facts: Designed by B. Marcus Priteca — opened August 17, 1920 as Pantages Theatre (vaudeville + film)
Sold to RKO in 1929, then to Warner Brothers — became Warner Bros. Downtown Theatre, debuting 'Gold Diggers of Broadway'
Closed 1975, used as a church, then converted to the Downtown Jewelry Exchange in the late 1980s
The original baroque theater interior is still intact INSIDE the jewelry exchange — red curtains, gold chandeliers, projectors visible from the balcony
The Warner Bros. emblem is still visible behind the current diamond motif above the corner marquee
Security is notoriously averse to photography — you can wander in during business hours but camera use is risky
One of the most extraordinary hidden-in-plain-sight spaces in LA

features.detail: 9-story building with corner marquee at 7th & Hill

features.text: Warner Bros. emblem behind diamond motif

features.detail: Original baroque theater interior visible inside jewelry exchange — red curtains, gold chandeliers, projectors
Pantages-era facade detailing

connections.link: Both on 7th Street, 130m apart — a movie palace hidden inside a jewelry exchange, a whiskey bar inside a jewelry emporium
DTLA's theme: magnificent spaces concealed behind mundane facades
Both opened during 7th Street's transformation into DTLA's premier commercial corridor

pois.name: Brockman Building
facts: Built 1912 for John Brockman — first building west of the Broadway Commercial District to reach the city's 150-foot height limit
Brockman's move started the westward expansion of DTLA's commercial district and turned 7th Street into the city's high-end retail corridor
Designed by George D. Barnett of Barnett, Haynes & Barnett — 12-story Beaux-Arts, Classical, and Romanesque Revival
Features elaborate terra cotta detailing and a copper cornice — the only copper cornice in the city at time of construction
J.W. Robinson Co. department store and other retailers followed Brockman westward along 7th Street
Converted to 80 condos in 2006-2008 — owner went bankrupt before completion

features.detail: 12-story Beaux-Arts facade with elaborate terra cotta detailing
Copper cornice — unique in LA at time of construction

connections.link: Directly across 7th Street — Brockman and Brock, two different men who both bet on 7th Street
Brockman opened the corridor, Brock followed 10 years later
One building decision created the gravitational pull that brought Brock, Bullock's, and the entire retail district westward

pois.name: Foreman & Clark Building
facts: Built 1929 for Foreman & Clark clothing retailer — their flagship store and corporate offices
Designed by Curlett & Beelman — superb example of Art Deco-Gothic architecture, combining both styles
17 stories — towered over the surrounding retail district
Foreman & Clark was headquartered here for approximately 30 years before Downtown's mid-century decline
Reopened 2023 as an adaptive reuse hotel — one of several 7th Street landmarks converted to hospitality

features.detail: 17-story Art Deco-Gothic tower with distinctive stepped profile
Gothic tracery combined with Art Deco geometric ornamentation

connections.link: Two clothing empires, built one year apart, within 2 blocks on 7th Street
Two competing interpretations of Art Deco — one married to Gothic, one to French glass

pois.name: Fine Arts Building
facts: 13-story Romanesque building designed by Walker & Eisen (same firm as the Oviatt Building) — built 1926 as studios and galleries for downtown artists
Two-story lobby with 17 bronze-and-glass display cases for artist-tenants to showcase their work — a 'living above the shop' arrangement
Walls decorated with ceramic relief panels by Ernest A. Batchelder — the nation's premiere decorative tile designer and critical force in the Arts and Crafts movement
Batchelder tile: small sculptural inserts, tile panels, and ornamental details throughout the lobby and common areas
Originally housed artist studios with skylights on upper floors
One of the few buildings in LA purpose-built for art production, not just display

features.detail: 13-story Romanesque facade
Batchelder tile throughout lobby — ceramic relief panels, small sculptural inserts
Two-story ornate lobby atrium

connections.link: Two buildings defined by a single artisan's lifework — Batchelder's American craft tile vs. Lalique's French crystal
Art production and art commerce, both on the same stretch of DTLA
Both opened in 1926 — Walker & Eisen designed one, Goodhue designed the other

pois.routeNote: At edge of 500m radius — supplemental POI

pois.name: Los Angeles Central Library
facts: Designed by New York architect Bertram Goodhue — his last project. He died of a heart attack in April 1924; associate Carleton Winslow completed it in 1926
Early example of Art Deco with Egyptian and Mediterranean Revival influences — modeled after Goodhue's Nebraska State Capitol
Central tower topped with a tiled mosaic pyramid with suns on the sides — the hand holding a torch at the apex represents the 'Light of Learning'
Sculptor Lee Lawrie created all exterior sculptures — same artist who later carved the Atlas at Rockefeller Center
Theme ('Light of Learning') devised by University of Nebraska philosophy professor Hartley Burr Alexander
Maguire Gardens adjacent to the library houses the World Peace Bell (2001) — cast from coins and medals donated by 103 countries, Japanese-style bell rung by striking the outside (no clapper)
1993 addition named for former mayor Tom Bradley

features.detail: Central tower with tiled mosaic pyramid and hand-holding-torch finial
Egyptian and Mediterranean Revival facade with Art Deco geometric forms
Lee Lawrie sculptures on exterior — same sculptor as Rockefeller Center Atlas
World Peace Bell in adjacent Maguire Gardens — Japanese-style, no clapper, rung from outside

connections.link: Same sculptor carved DTLA's library and NYC's most famous statue
Both opened 1926 — the year DTLA's cultural institutions crystallized
Both are Art Deco monuments to aspiration — one for knowledge, one for style

pois.routeNote: North of bar — supplemental POI, not on main route but within radius

pois.name: The Biltmore Los Angeles
facts: Opened October 1, 1923 — largest hotel west of Chicago when built. Cost: just under $9 million
Opening gala: 3,000 guests, seven courses, seven orchestras, caged canaries providing birdsong
Designed by Schultze & Weaver (same firm as New York's Waldorf-Astoria) in Beaux Arts / Spanish-Italian Renaissance Revival
5,200 tons of steel in the frame alone
Italian artist John B. Smeraldi spent 7 months painting Greco-Roman frescoes — satyrs, cherubs, nymphs across the ceilings
Hosted Academy Awards ceremonies in the mid-to-late 1930s — Clark Gable and Ginger Rogers both won their Oscars here
Elizabeth Short (the 'Black Dahlia') was last seen alive in the Biltmore lobby, January 1947 — her body found the next day, bisected at the waist. Case remains unsolved
Her ghost reportedly haunts the lobby and the 10th and 11th floors
Built in 18 months

features.detail: Massive Beaux Arts / Renaissance Revival facade on Grand Avenue
Ornate lobby with Smeraldi ceiling frescoes — satyrs, cherubs, nymphs
Spanish-Italian Renaissance Revival ornamentation on exterior

connections.link: Opened within a year of each other — the hotel and the bank that anchored Olive Street
Two buildings by elite architectural firms, 2 blocks apart, competing for DTLA's most opulent interior
Last seen alive in the lobby — the most infamous unsolved murder in LA history

pois.name: Palace Theatre
facts: Oldest remaining original Orpheum theater in the United States — opened 1911
Designed by G. Albert Lansburgh and Robert Brown Young
Home of LA's Orpheum Circuit — Harry Houdini, Will Rogers, Fred Astaire, and Rita Hayworth all performed here
Converted to silent movie theater 'Broadway Palace' in 1926, Fox West Coast Theatres took over in 1929
$1 million restoration in 2010, reopened as live venue in June 2011

features.detail: Ornate theater facade on Broadway

connections.link: Houdini performed on this stage — the oldest surviving Orpheum in America
Entertainment and commerce expanding simultaneously in early DTLA

stop1_losAngelesTheatre.notes: Chaplin + Einstein premiere is a natural 3-hint narrative riddle. Route 66 terminus marker is a spotter in the sidewalk. 'This corner was once the busiest in the world — 504,000 people a day' is a reframe. The 12 movie palaces on Broadway support a sequence clue. Clifton's 'pay what you wish' policy is a connector to the bar's own building history.

stop2_oviattBuilding.notes: Lalique glass doors (two angels holding a mission bell) are spotter gold — observable, specific, countable. '30 tons of glass shipped from France' is a cipher-lite number. 'A haberdasher saw one exhibition in Paris and changed the LA skyline' is a reframe. Spring Street as 'Wall Street of the West' (named for a love interest) is a narrative riddle. St. Vincent Court's transformation from Jesuit college to kebab alley is a connector to the theme of DTLA reinvention.

stop3_brockBuilding.notes: The building's journey (jewelry emporium → cafeteria → whiskey bar) is a perfect sequence. 'The same display cases that once held diamonds now hold whiskey' is THE reframe for the entire hunt. The Bank of Italy building next door (Giannini lending to immigrants) is a narrative riddle. Brockman vs. Brock — two different men, same street, 10 years apart — is an elimination/connector. '$1,600/month for 25,000 sq ft' is a cipher-lite anchor.

overpassBuildingStock.note: Overpass returned limited dated buildings due to server load — the area has hundreds of pre-war structures. Key dated buildings from Overpass: Fine Arts Building (1926), LA Central Library (1926), Los Angeles Theatre (1930), James Oviatt Building (undated in OSM but confirmed 1928).

neighborhoodContext.7thStreetCorridor: 7th Street between Broadway and Figueroa was DTLA's upscale retail district from 1912 (Brockman Building) through the 1960s. Bullock's department store occupied an entire block (Broadway to Hill). The corridor's decline mirrors DTLA's mid-century hollowing out; its revival (Seven Grand, hotel conversions) mirrors the current renaissance.
neighborhoodContext.broadwayTheaterDistrict: 12 movie palaces built 1911-1931 between 3rd and 9th on Broadway. First and largest theater district on the National Register (1979). Still the densest concentration of historic theaters in the world.
neighborhoodContext.springStreetFinancialDistrict: 23 financial structures, 'Wall Street of the West.' Named for a love interest. Now Gallery Row. One block east of the bar.
neighborhoodContext.route66Connection: The original 1926 western terminus of Route 66 was at 7th & Broadway — one block east of the bar's building. Every Dust Bowl migrant, every dreamer driving west, ended their journey within walking distance of where Seven Grand now stands.

Oviatt Building Lalique glass doors — confirm visible from street without entering
St. Vincent Court — confirm current restaurant/shop mix and accessibility
Warner Bros. emblem above Jewelry Exchange marquee — confirm still visible
Brock Building swan's neck pediment — confirm visible from street level
Pershing Square statues — confirm locations given ongoing Olympic renovation
Seven Grand exterior signage — confirm current sign text and placement
